metrics.narrative: The business problem is confirmed and specific: Grupo Bimbo has no spend visibility by category or region, forcing manual inquiries to regional procurement teams to identify top suppliers (Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10: 'I don't know who my top 10 flour suppliers are in a certain region. To find out, I have to go to that region, to the procurement team, and they have to do an analysis for me'). Supplier evaluation is manual (Excel/PowerPoint), contract management is fragmented by region (Latin America uses Agiloft, North America uses ContractLogix, EMEA and Asia use different tools), and there is no centralized contract expiration alerting. The pain is operational (manual work, data silos, no cross-regional visibility) and strategic (cannot execute category planning, tail spend management, or procurement maturity without foundational data). However, no quantified financial impact has been articulated: no savings target, no FTE reduction goal, no cycle-time improvement metric, and no ROI benchmark stated. The business case Mario proposed (to secure budget via POC) would quantify this, but it has not been built after 4 months. Sources: 2026-02-10 call (Mario Delacruz quotes above), 2025-08-28 discovery call (fragmented CLM, manual sourcing processes noted).

champion.narrative: Mario Delacruz (Mexico City, Global Procurement Compliance Director, mario.delacruz@grupobimbo.com) is the confirmed champion. He is engaged, collaborative, has deep access to the buying committee (reports to the relevant stakeholder, COE Global Lead; works with Hector Ibancovichi, Lina Parra, Hayde Romero; and has direct line to David Hernandez, CPO), and has articulated clear priorities, pain, and a path forward (POC → business case → budget approval). Evidence of advocacy: Mario drove the 2025-08-28 discovery call (brought Claudia Camacho, Hector Ibancovichi, Lina Parra, Hayde Romero), defined the three priorities on 2026-02-10 (Spend Analytics, SRM, CLM), proposed the POC and business case approach, committed to brief the relevant stakeholder and engage regional COEs, and accepted the 2026-02-17 follow-up meeting and the 2026-03-04 Zycus event invitation. However, Mario is a champion WITHOUT AUTHORITY: he has no budget, cannot approve the business case alone, and cannot drive the deal forward without David Hernandez (CPO) and Claudia Camacho (his manager) actively sponsoring it. The 4-month stall (2026-02-10 to 2026-06-12) with no POC execution, no business case delivery, and no follow-up meeting signals that Mario has not been able to secure internal alignment or executive sponsorship, and his engagement is COOLING. The 2026-06-12 Horizon invite (rep-initiated, no reply recorded) is a re-engagement attempt, but Mario has not responded. The risk is that without executive air cover (David Hernandez engaged) and a formal evaluation timeline, Mario's enthusiasm will fade and the deal will drift. Current engagement: PASSIVE / COOLING. Champion strength: DEVELOPING (engaged and collaborative, but lacks authority and is stalled without executive sponsorship).

competition.narrative: The competitive landscape is CLEAR and recency-weighted. GEP is the strongest current threat: GEP Smart is live and incumbent for sourcing ('working well' per Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10), and GEP is also under evaluation for Spend Analytics (the #1 priority where Zycus is competing). The 4-month stall has given GEP time to consolidate their position, and the risk is that Grupo Bimbo defaults to expanding GEP (path of least resistance, existing relationship, user adoption) rather than introducing Zycus as a new vendor. GEP's weakness: standalone deployment with no ERP integration (Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10: 'GEP Smart works well but it's standalone, not connected to the relevant stakeholder manually populate sourcing events. That's the challenge'). Zycus wedge: end-to-end S2P integration (single code base, Oracle ERP integration, unified data model) and AI/Agentic AI differentiation (Merlin platform for contract redlining, spend intelligence, supplier evaluation automation). Coupa was named as under exploration on the 2025-08-28 call (Mario Delacruz: 'We are testing GEP and exploring others like Coupa') but has not been mentioned since and is DORMANT (no active evaluation, no recent reference). Oracle S2P is the incumbent ERP suite but is explicitly rejected as inadequate (Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10: 'Oracle is the official ERP, but Oracle S2P is not the best solution in the market. That's why Hector asked for this meeting'). Oracle's status: DO_NOTHING baseline (replaced by best-of-breed, not a competitive threat). The single strongest CURRENT threat is GEP, and the wedge is integration + AI.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational and financial pain is confirmed, specific, and articulated by the champion (Mario Delacruz) with cross-functional impact. Pain #1: No spend visibility by category or region. Mario Delacruz (2026-02-10) stated, 'I don't know who my top 10 flour suppliers are in a certain region. To find out, I have to go to that region, to the procurement team, and they have to do an analysis for me. I can't filter flour category for Colombia 2025 and instantly see the top 10 suppliers.' This blocks category planning, tail spend management, and procurement maturity. Pain owner: Mario Delacruz (Compliance Director) and the regional procurement COEs who execute manual inquiries. Pain #2: Manual supplier evaluation. Mario Delacruz (2026-02-10): 'Last year we had to do a supplier evaluation. We have no tool, nothing of the sort. Our favorite tool was Excel. You can imagine. It was done in Excel and PowerPoint. We had to ask R&D, Quality, Innovation to define their KPIs. Collecting everything, making sure everyone sent their inputs… it was a lot.' This creates audit risk, inconsistent supplier scoring, and slow cycle time. Pain owner: Mario Delacruz (SRM lead) and cross-functional teams (Quality, R&D, Innovation). Pain #3: Fragmented contract management. Mario Delacruz (2026-02-10): 'We don't have a centralized CLM solution. Latin America uses Agiloft, North America uses ContractLogix, EMEA uses something different, Asia uses something different. I don't have contract expiration alerts. If I need to know when my sugar contract in Colombia expires, I can't see it.' This blocks contract visibility, renewal management, and legal efficiency. Pain owner: Legal (who will lead CLM) and Procurement (who need visibility). Pain #4: GEP Smart and Oracle operate standalone with no integration. Mario Delacruz (2026-02-10): 'GEP Smart is working well for sourcing, but it's standalone. It's not connected to our ERP. We manually populate sourcing events. That's the challenge.' This creates data silos, manual rework, and blocks end-to-end S2P automation. Pain owner: Mario Delacruz and regional procurement teams. The pain is confirmed and quantified operationally, but no financial impact (cost, FTE, savings) has been quantified. The business case Mario proposed would quantify ROI, but it has not been delivered.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, InfoSec requirements, and procurement approval workflows have not been discussed. No MSA, SOW, Order Form, or legal template has been referenced. No InfoSec questionnaire, data residency requirement, or security review process has been stated. The Oracle ERP integration requirement is confirmed (2026-02-10 call: 'Oracle is the official ERP, we are not changing it, but Oracle S2P is not the best and we want best-of-breed integrated with Oracle'), but the technical integration scoping, IT approval process, and implementation timeline have not been defined. The 2026-02-17 follow-up meeting was intended to include a Zycus engineer for integration scoping, but the meeting did not occur. Legal's role in CLM implementation is confirmed (Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10: 'Legal will lead CLM, we will provide requirements'), but Legal has not been engaged by Zycus and their approval process is undefined. No partner or SI involvement has been mentioned. The paper process is a knowledge gap because the deal is pre-budget and pre-commercial; contracting and legal mechanics will surface only after budget approval and POC completion. The next move is to co-author a mutual evaluation and contracting plan with Mario Delacruz and Claudia Camacho that maps the approval chain (Procurement → Legal → IT → Finance → David Hernandez), integration requirements, and timeline milestones, then present it to David Hernandez for executive sign-off.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is emerging but not formalized. The 2025-08-28 discovery call showed broad stakeholder participation (Claudia Camacho, COE Global Lead; Mario Delacruz, Compliance Director; Hector Ibancovichi, Agribusiness Director & Decision Maker contact role; Lina Parra, CRM Lead; Hayde Romero, Oracle Cloud Procurement Manager), indicating a multi-functional evaluation team. The 2026-02-10 call clarified the approval chain: (1) Mario Delacruz (Champion) must build a business case demonstrating ROI to secure budget; (2) Claudia Camacho (his manager, COE Global Lead) is a gatekeeper and must be briefed and aligned (Mario committed to a 1:1 with her on 2026-02-11 to discuss Zycus); (3) Regional COEs must be engaged and provide input (Mario committed to engage them before the 2026-02-17 follow-up); (4) David Hernandez (CPO, Economic Buyer) is the ultimate decision authority and budget approver, but he has not been engaged; (5) Legal leads CLM implementation and will have veto authority on that module, but Procurement (Mario/Claudia) must provide requirements; (6) Finance's role in budget approval is undefined but implicit (business case approval likely requires Finance sign-off). The buyer has no formal evaluation timeline, no RFP process, no vendor scoring framework, and no decision deadline stated. The 4-month stall (2026-02-10 to 2026-06-12) with no POC execution, no business case delivery, and no follow-up meeting suggests that internal alignment (Mario → Claudia → COEs → David Hernandez) has not been achieved and the decision process has not progressed. The risk is that fragmented authority (Legal, Procurement, COEs, Finance) without a centralized executive sponsor (David Hernandez engaged) will cause indefinite delay.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are emerging from the three stated priorities and the pain points articulated, but they have not been formalized into a written RFP, BRD, or decision matrix. From the 2026-02-10 call, Mario Delacruz defined success criteria implicitly: (1) Spend Analytics must provide true analytical capability (filter by category, region, supplier) versus Oracle FDI's reporting-only model ('I need to filter flour category for Colombia 2025 and see the top 10 suppliers instantly'); (2) SRM must enable cross-functional supplier evaluation (Quality, R&D, Innovation teams defining KPIs) and replace manual Excel/PowerPoint workflows; (3) CLM must centralize contract visibility across regions (Latin America, North America, EMEA, Asia) and provide automated expiration alerts and redlining powered by AI; (4) Integration with the relevant stakeholder and GEP Smart is non-negotiable (standalone tools are explicitly rejected as inadequate); (5) AI/Agentic AI capability is a stated priority from the CPO (David Hernandez) but specific AI use cases are undefined ('We want AI but we don't know for what processes'). The criteria also include data governance as a foundation (Mario Delacruz, 2026-02-10: 'We have to start from a good database, good data governance, good system input, so AI can give us good output'). However, the formal decision process, scoring methodology, vendor comparison framework, and Legal/Finance approval criteria have not been documented. The POC that Mario proposed (to demonstrate spend visibility and build the business case) would validate fit against these implicit criteria, but it has not been executed. The next move is to co-author a formal evaluation framework with Mario Delacruz and Claudia Camacho, then secure David Hernandez's sign-off.

north_star_verdict.headline: Strong discovery with quantified pain across Spend Analytics, SRM, and CLM, but stalled 4+ months awaiting POC execution and business case approval. No Economic Buyer engagement, no budget, and GEP incumbent expanding while Zycus waits for champion follow-through.

flags: GEP incumbency advantage is growing while Zycus stalls. GEP Smart is live for sourcing ('working well'), GEP is under evaluation for Spend Analytics (the #1 priority where Zycus competes), and the 4-month engagement gap has given GEP time to consolidate their position with users, stakeholders, and the buyer's trust. The risk is that Grupo Bimbo defaults to expanding GEP (path of least resistance, existing relationship) rather than introducing Zycus as a new vendor. The wedge is integration (GEP standalone vs Zycus end-to-end S2P with the relevant stakeholder integration) and AI differentiation (Merlin Agentic AI for contract redlining, spend intelligence, SRM automation). The next move is to execute the Spend Analytics POC Mario proposed and demonstrate integration + AI ROI in the business case before GEP formalizes their Spend Analytics deployment. Speed matters: every additional month of delay strengthens GEP's incumbent position.
best_practice_check.summary: Champion is engaged but lacks authority; executive sponsorship (David Hernandez, CPO) is the unlock. GEP incumbency is growing during the 4-month stall. POC execution and business case delivery are stalled, blocking budget approval and progression.

competitive_position.summary: GEP is the strongest current threat: incumbent for sourcing (GEP Smart live and 'working well'), also under evaluation for Spend Analytics (the #1 priority where Zycus competes). The 4-month stall (2026-02-10 to 2026-06-12) with no Zycus POC has given GEP time to consolidate their position, and the risk is that Grupo Bimbo defaults to expanding GEP (existing relationship, user adoption, path of least resistance) rather than introducing Zycus. GEP's weakness: standalone deployment with no ERP integration, manual sourcing event population. Zycus wedge: end-to-end S2P integration (single code base, Oracle ERP integration, unified data model) and AI/Agentic AI differentiation (Merlin for contract redlining, spend intelligence, supplier evaluation automation). Coupa was explored in 2025 but is dormant (no recent mention). Oracle S2P is the do-nothing baseline, explicitly rejected as inadequate.

Position Zycus as the single integrated platform (Spend Analytics + SRM + CLM + AI) versus GEP's piecemeal approach. Neutralize GEP's incumbency by demonstrating integration ROI in the business case.

Not a competitive threat; position Zycus as the best-of-breed S2P layer that integrates to Oracle ERP (the buyer's requirement) rather than replacing it. Oracle's weakness is Zycus's opportunity.

deltas.subject: The POC and business case are stalled for 4 months with no delivery and no clear path to budget approval. Mario Delacruz proposed a Spend Analytics POC on 2026-02-10 to demonstrate ROI and build the business case, committed to brief the relevant stakeholder and engage regional COEs, and scheduled a 2026-02-17 follow-up with a Zycus engineer for scoping. Without POC execution, Mario cannot quantify ROI, and without the business case, he cannot secure budget approval from David Hernandez and Finance. Position Zycus as the single integrated platform (Spend Analytics + SRM + CLM + AI) versus GEP's piecemeal approach. Neutralize GEP's incumbency by demonstrating integration ROI in the business case.

value.name: GEP
value.quote: We are using GEP Smart for all the organizations, all the business units, and it's working well. Honestly, it's working. But it's standalone — not connected to my ERP. GEP Smart is a temporary solution; I'm planning to use Oracle sourcing for the complete source-to-pay process.
value.how_we_win: Exploit GEP's integration gap: position Zycus as the best-of-breed layer that connects GEP, Oracle, and other systems into an end-to-end S2P flow without ripping out what works. Lead with Merlin AI agents for spend analytics (the #1 priority where GEP is also under eval) to differentiate on automation and insight, not just integration plumbing. Prove it in a POC before GEP's incumbency advantage locks the buyer into their standalone model.
